content:
I Thought My Incognito Mode Made Me Invisible. A Privacy Researcher Proved I Was Naked in 12 Minutes.

9 min read
Just now

--

I used to think incognito mode was my digital invisibility cloak.
Not NSA-proof. But good enough. I had the little hat-and-glasses icon. I had the dark browser window. I had that satisfying feeling of closing the tab and watching my history vanish like it never happened. I read the one-line description once — “browse privately” — and figured I was probably fine.
Then last Thursday, I watched a privacy researcher in Berlin map every website I visited, build a fingerprint that uniquely identified my laptop, and cross-reference my “private” browsing session with my “normal” session from three days earlier.
It took her twelve minutes.
And she was doing it for free, as a favor, because she “thought I’d find it interesting.”
I did not find it interesting. I found it horrifying.
The Setup: Confidence Before the Fall
I’ve been using Chrome’s incognito mode for years. It was my ritual. Job hunting on the work laptop? Incognito. Shopping for gifts my wife might see in the history? Incognito. Reading articles I didn’t want in my “recently viewed” when I screenshared in meetings? Incognito.
I was proud of my system. I had a whole mental model. Normal mode = tracked. Incognito mode = clean slate. The dark window felt like a protective shield. Like a…
